[[File:Ototachibanahime.png|thumb|Ototachibanahime (from ''{{ill|Zenkengojitsu|ja|前賢故実}}'')]] '''Ototachibanahime''' (弟橘媛, birth unknown – 40th year of Emperor Keikō) was, according to the ''{{ill|Nihon Shoki|ja|日本書紀}}'', the consort of {{ill|Yamato Takeru|ja|ヤマトタケル}}. In the ''{{ill|Kojiki|ja|古事記}}'', she is referred to as Otachibanahime no Mikoto, the consort of Yamato Takeru. == Ototachibanahime in the Chronicles == According to the ''Nihon Shoki'', she was the daughter of {{ill|Takeoshi Yama Tarine|ja|建忍山垂根}} of the {{ill|Hozumi clan|ja|穂積氏}} and bore a son, {{ill|Prince Wakatarashihiko|ja|稚武彦王}}, with Yamato Takeru.''Nihon Shoki'' (translated by {{ill|Mitsuo Ueda|ja|宇治谷孟}}), p. 174 {{Quotation|...When they reached Sagami and intended to cross to Kazusa, Yamato Takeru declared, “This small sea can be easily leapt over.” However, halfway through the journey, a violent storm arose, and the ship began to drift. One of the consorts accompanying him, named Ototachibanahime, daughter of Takeoshi Yama Tarine, addressed him, saying, “This storm must be the work of the sea deity. I will take your place and appease the gods.” With those words, she leapt into the sea. The storm subsided immediately, and the ship safely reached shore. The place was later called ''Tachimizu'' in her honor...''Nihon Shoki'' (translated by Mitsuo Ueda), p. 168}} The {{ill|Tachibana Shrine (Mobara)|ja|橘樹神社 (茂原市)}} in {{ill|Mobara|ja|茂原市}}, Chiba Prefecture, is said to be where Yamato Takeru commemorated Ototachibanahime by planting a tachibana (citrus) tree as her grave marker. This shrine is a significant site, being listed in the ''Engishiki'' as one of the principal shrines of Kazusa Province and the only shrine officially dedicated to Ototachibanahime. == ''Kojiki'' Narrative == The ''Kojiki'' recounts a similar story of Ototachibanahime's sacrifice during Yamato Takeru's journey. When a storm arose in the [[Uraga Channel]], she offered herself to calm the sea: {{Quotation|...Then, as they attempted to cross the sea at Hashirimizu, a storm arose. To appease the wrath of the sea deity, Ototachibanahime declared, “I will take my lord’s place to calm the sea and ensure the success of the mission.” She laid eight layers of sedge mats, skin mats, and silk mats on the waves, seated herself on them, and entered the sea. The waves subsided, allowing the ship to proceed.''Kojiki'' (translated by {{ill|Makoto Tsuda|ja|次田真幸}}), p. 150}} Her parting song, only found in the ''Kojiki,'' reflects her gratitude towards Yamato Takeru for his concern during their peril: * ''As the fire blazes on Saho's field, you asked of me amidst the flames. My lord, how kind you are!''''Kojiki'' (translated by Makoto Tsuda), p. 147 == ''Hitachi Fudoki'' Account == In the ''{{ill|Hitachi Fudoki|ja|常陸国風土記}}'', Ototachibanahime appears as ''Otachibanahime no Mikoto'' or ''Tachibana Kōgō'' (Empress Tachibana). Yamato Takeru, referred to as Emperor Yamato Takeru, is depicted reuniting with her in the area now known as Itako and Namegata in Ibaraki Prefecture.''Fudoki,'' {{ill|New Japanese Classic Literature Complete Works|ja|新編日本古典文学全集}}, p. 388 == Shrines and Legends == Shrines dedicated to Ototachibanahime, such as {{ill|Tachibana Shrine (Mobara)|ja|橘樹神社 (茂原市)}} and {{ill|Hashirimizu Shrine|ja|走水神社}}, are located in Chiba Prefecture and other regions. Legends surrounding her, including the origin of place names like ''Sodegaura'' in Chiba and Narashino, testify to her lasting cultural significance. == References == {{Reflist}} == Bibliography == * Mitsuo Ueda, ''Nihon Shoki (Volume I): Full Modern Translation,'' Kodansha, 1988. ISBN 4-06-158833-8 * Makoto Tsuda, ''Kojiki (Volume II): Full Annotated Translation,'' Kodansha, 1980. ISBN 4-06-158208-9 * Toshiwo Kikuchi, ''History of Tokyo Bay,'' Dai-Nihon Printing, 1974 * Ichiyuki Ono et al., ''Biographical Dictionary of Ancient and Medieval Japan,'' Tokyo-do Publishing, 2004. == See Also == * {{ill|Azuma|ja|東#呼称}} * {{ill|Tamasaki Shrine|ja|玉崎神社}} * {{ill|Ane Saki Shrine|ja|姉埼神社}} * {{ill|Soga Hime Shrine|ja|蘇我比咩神社}} * {{ill|Kisarazu Tower|ja|きみさらずタワー}} [[Category:unsorted To appease the wrath of the sea deity, Ototachibanahime declared, “I will take my lord’s place to calm the sea and ensure the success of the mission.” She laid eight layers of sedge mats, skin mats, and silk mats on the waves, seated herself on them, and entered the sea. The waves subsided, allowing the ship to proceed.''Kojiki'' (translated by {{ill|Makoto Tsuda|ja|次田真幸}}), p. 150}} Her parting song, only found in the ''Kojiki,'' reflects her gratitude towards Yamato Takeru for his concern during their peril: * ''As the fire blazes on Saho's field, you asked of me amidst the flames. My lord, how kind you are!''''Kojiki'' (translated by Makoto Tsuda), p. 147 == ''Hitachi Fudoki'' Account == In the ''{{ill|Hitachi Fudoki|ja|常陸国風土記}}'', Ototachibanahime appears as ''Otachibanahime no Mikoto'' or ''Tachibana Kōgō'' (Empress Tachibana). Yamato Takeru, referred to as Emperor Yamato Takeru, is depicted reuniting with her in the area now known as Itako and Namegata in Ibaraki Prefecture.''Fudoki,'' {{ill|New Japanese Classic Literature Complete Works|ja|新編日本古典文学全集}}, p. 388 == Shrines and Legends == Shrines dedicated to Ototachibanahime, such as {{ill|Tachibana Shrine (Mobara)|ja|橘樹神社 (茂原市)}} and {{ill|Hashirimizu Shrine|ja|走水神社}}, are located in Chiba Prefecture and other regions. Legends surrounding her, including the origin of place names like ''Sodegaura'' in Chiba and Narashino, testify to her lasting cultural significance. == References == {{Reflist}} == Bibliography == * Mitsuo Ueda, ''Nihon Shoki (Volume I): Full Modern Translation,'' Kodansha, 1988. ISBN 4-06-158833-8 * Makoto Tsuda, ''Kojiki (Volume II): Full Annotated Translation,'' Kodansha, 1980. ISBN 4-06-158208-9 * Toshiwo Kikuchi, ''History of Tokyo Bay,'' Dai-Nihon Printing, 1974 * Ichiyuki Ono et al., ''Biographical Dictionary of Ancient and Medieval Japan,'' Tokyo-do Publishing, 2004. == [[ja:弟橘媛]] '''Ototachibana-hime''' (弟橘媛) was the wife of {{ill|Yamato Takeru|ja|ヤマトタケル}}, according to the {{ill|Nihon Shoki|ja|日本書紀}}. In the {{ill|Kojiki|ja|古事記}}, she is referred to as Otachibana-hime no Mikoto (弟橘比売命), the consort of Yamato Takeru. == Ototachibana-hime in the Kojiki and Nihon Shoki == In the {{ill|Nihon Shoki|ja|日本書紀}}, she is described as the daughter of {{ill|Oshiyama-no-Sukune|ja|忍山宿禰}}, an ancestor of the {{ill|Hozumi clan|ja|穂積氏}}. She bore Yamato Takeru a son, {{ill|Wakatakehiko-no-Mikoto|ja|稚武彦王}}.宇治谷孟 『日本書紀（上）全現代語訳』 174ページ {{Quotation|While crossing the sea from Sagami to Kazusa, Yamato Takeru boasted, “This small sea, I could leap over it.” However, a violent storm arose, and the ship began to drift. Ototachibana-hime, his consort, said, “This storm must be the work of the sea god. Allow me to enter the sea as your substitute.” With these words, she entered the waves, and the storm ceased. The ship safely reached the shore, and the sea was named Tachibana Sea in her honor.宇治谷孟 『日本書紀（上）全現代語訳』 168ページ}} In {{ill|Mobara, Chiba|ja|茂原市}}, {{ill|Tachibana Shrine (Mobara)|ja|橘樹神社}} honors Ototachibana-hime. The shrine is an {{ill|Engishiki|ja|延喜式}}-listed shrine, one of Kazusa's five major shrines, and the only shrine recorded in historical texts to enshrine her. According to legend, Yamato Takeru planted a tachibana tree as her grave marker here.菊池利夫 『東京湾史』 80ページ In the {{ill|Kojiki|ja|古事記}}, Ototachibana-hime offered herself to calm the sea gods’ wrath as Yamato Takeru encountered a storm at Hashirimizu. She placed eight layers of sedge, hide, and silk mats on the waves, sat on them, and entered the sea, calming the waters.次田真幸 『古事記（中）全訳注』 150ページ {{Quotation|She said, “I shall enter the sea as your substitute. Please proceed safely with your campaign.” She then spread eight layers of sedge, hide, and silk mats over the waves, seated herself on them, and entered the sea. The storm subsided, and the ship advanced. As she descended, she sang:
* “In Aibu’s fields, flames rise; standing amid them, you questioned me, my lord.”
Her comb was found washed ashore seven days later, and Yamato Takeru enshrined it, creating her tomb.次田真幸 『古事記（中）全訳注』 147ページ}} The comb is said to be enshrined at Tachibana Shrine, and her poem expresses her gratitude to Yamato Takeru for his care during a previous ordeal. Yamato Takeru’s lament, “Azuma haya” (“My wife!”), upon reaching Azuma, is said to have named the eastern provinces “Azuma,” a geonymic tale.宇治谷孟 『日本書紀（上）全現代語訳』 169ページ次田真幸 『古事記（中）全訳注』 153ページ == Hitachi no Kuni Fudoki == In the {{ill|Hitachi no Kuni Fudoki|ja|常陸国風土記}}, Ototachibana-hime appears as Ōtachibana-hime or Empress Tachibana, while Yamato Takeru is referred to as Emperor Yamato.植垣節也 『風土記 新編日本古典文学全集5』 388ページ == Sendaikujihongi == In the {{ill|Sendaikujihongi|ja|先代旧事本紀}}, Ototachibana-hime is recorded as the mother of Wakatakehiko-no-Mikoto and eight other sons.植垣節也 『風土記 新編日本古典文学全集5』 417ページ == Shrines and Legends == Ototachibana-hime’s legend is central to shrines across Kazusa (modern-day Chiba) and beyond. Shrines such as {{ill|Tachibana Shrine (Kanagawa)|ja|橘樹神社}}, {{ill|Nomura Shrine|ja|能褒野神社}}, and {{ill|Hashirimizu Shrine|ja|走水神社 (横須賀市)}} enshrine her alongside Yamato Takeru. Places like {{ill|Sodegaura|ja|袖ケ浦}} are named after the legend of her sleeves washing ashore. The sacred tachibana tree associated with her suggests she may have been a shaman.小野一之・鈴木彰・谷口榮・樋口州男 『人物伝小辞典 古代・中世編』 54ページ == References == == Bibliography == * 宇治谷孟 『日本書紀（上）全現代語訳』 講談社、1988年 ISBN 4-06-158833-8 * 次田真幸 『古事記（中）全訳注』 講談社、1980年 ISBN 4-06-158208-9 * 植垣節也 『風土記 新編日本古典文学全集5』 小学館、1997年 ISBN 4-09-658005-8 * 菊池利夫 『東京湾史』 大日本図書印刷 1974年 * 小野一之・鈴木彰・谷口榮・樋口州男 『人物伝小辞典 古代・中世編』 東京堂出版 2004年 == Related Topics == * {{ill|Azuma|ja|東#呼称}} * {{ill|Tamasaki Shrine|ja|玉崎神社}} * {{ill|Ane Saki Shrine|ja|姉埼神社}} * {{ill|Soga-hime Shrine|ja|蘇我比咩神社}} * {{ill|Kimisara Tower|ja|きみさらずタワー}} {{Japan mythology}} [[Category:Ototachibana-hime]] [[Category:Women in ancient Japan]] [[Category:Consorts of Yamato Takeru]] [[Category:Legendary Japanese women]] [[Category:Japanese deities (human-related)]] [[Category:Birth and death dates unknown]] [[Category:unsorted link categories]][[category:unsorted completion categories]] [[Category:kk]] [[Category:kk]][[category:kk]] [[Category:attempting to force efresh]] [[Category:bump-01-18]] [[ja:弟橘媛]]

[[File:Ototachibanahime.png|thumb|Ototachibanahime (from ''{{ill|Zenkengojitsu|ja|前賢故実}}'')]] '''Ototachibanahime''' (弟橘媛, birth unknown – 40th year of Emperor Keikō) was, according to the ''{{ill|Nihon Shoki|ja|日本書紀}}'', the consort of {{ill|Yamato Takeru|ja|ヤマトタケル}}. In the ''{{ill|Kojiki|ja|古事記}}'', she is referred to as Otachibanahime no Mikoto, the consort of Yamato Takeru. == Ototachibanahime in the Chronicles == According to the ''Nihon Shoki'', she was the daughter of {{ill|Takeoshi Yama Tarine|ja|建忍山垂根}} of the {{ill|Hozumi clan|ja|穂積氏}} and bore a son, {{ill|Prince Wakatarashihiko|ja|稚武彦王}}, with Yamato Takeru.''Nihon Shoki'' (translated by {{ill|Mitsuo Ueda|ja|宇治谷孟}}), p. 174 {{Quotation|...When they reached Sagami and intended to cross to Kazusa, Yamato Takeru declared, “This small sea can be easily leapt over.” However, halfway through the journey, a violent storm arose, and the ship began to drift. One of the consorts accompanying him, named Ototachibanahime, daughter of Takeoshi Yama Tarine, addressed him, saying, “This storm must be the work of the sea deity. I will take your place and appease the gods.” With those words, she leapt into the sea. The storm subsided immediately, and the ship safely reached shore. The place was later called ''Tachimizu'' in her honor...''Nihon Shoki'' (translated by Mitsuo Ueda), p. 168}} The {{ill|Tachibana Shrine (Mobara)|ja|橘樹神社 (茂原市)}} in {{ill|Mobara|ja|茂原市}}, Chiba Prefecture, is said to be where Yamato Takeru commemorated Ototachibanahime by planting a tachibana (citrus) tree as her grave marker. This shrine is a significant site, being listed in the ''Engishiki'' as one of the principal shrines of Kazusa Province and the only shrine officially dedicated to Ototachibanahime. == ''Kojiki'' Narrative == The ''Kojiki'' recounts a similar story of Ototachibanahime's sacrifice during Yamato Takeru's journey. When a storm arose in the [[Uraga Channel]], she offered herself to calm the sea: {{Quotation|...Then, as they attempted to cross the sea at Hashirimizu, a storm arose. To appease the wrath of the sea deity, Ototachibanahime declared, “I will take my lord’s place to calm the sea and ensure the success of the mission.” She laid eight layers of sedge mats, skin mats, and silk mats on the waves, seated herself on them, and entered the sea. The waves subsided, allowing the ship to proceed.''Kojiki'' (translated by {{ill|Makoto Tsuda|ja|次田真幸}}), p. 150}} Her parting song, only found in the ''Kojiki,'' reflects her gratitude towards Yamato Takeru for his concern during their peril: * ''As the fire blazes on Saho's field, you asked of me amidst the flames. My lord, how kind you are!''''Kojiki'' (translated by Makoto Tsuda), p. 147 == ''Hitachi Fudoki'' Account == In the ''{{ill|Hitachi Fudoki|ja|常陸国風土記}}'', Ototachibanahime appears as ''Otachibanahime no Mikoto'' or ''Tachibana Kōgō'' (Empress Tachibana). Yamato Takeru, referred to as Emperor Yamato Takeru, is depicted reuniting with her in the area now known as Itako and Namegata in Ibaraki Prefecture.''Fudoki,'' {{ill|New Japanese Classic Literature Complete Works|ja|新編日本古典文学全集}}, p. 388 == Shrines and Legends == Shrines dedicated to Ototachibanahime, such as {{ill|Tachibana Shrine (Mobara)|ja|橘樹神社 (茂原市)}} and {{ill|Hashirimizu Shrine|ja|走水神社}}, are located in Chiba Prefecture and other regions. Legends surrounding her, including the origin of place names like ''Sodegaura'' in Chiba and Narashino, testify to her lasting cultural significance. == References == {{Reflist}} == Bibliography == * Mitsuo Ueda, ''Nihon Shoki (Volume I): Full Modern Translation,'' Kodansha, 1988. ISBN 4-06-158833-8 * Makoto Tsuda, ''Kojiki (Volume II): Full Annotated Translation,'' Kodansha, 1980. ISBN 4-06-158208-9 * Toshiwo Kikuchi, ''History of Tokyo Bay,'' Dai-Nihon Printing, 1974 * Ichiyuki Ono et al., ''Biographical Dictionary of Ancient and Medieval Japan,'' Tokyo-do Publishing, 2004. == Please proceed safely with your campaign.” She then spread eight layers of sedge, hide, and silk mats over the waves, seated herself on them, and entered the sea. The storm subsided, and the ship advanced. As she descended, she sang:
* “In Aibu’s fields, flames rise; standing amid them, you questioned me, my lord.”
Her comb was found washed ashore seven days later, and Yamato Takeru enshrined it, creating her tomb.次田真幸 『古事記（中）全訳注』 147ページ}} The comb is said to be enshrined at Tachibana Shrine, and her poem expresses her gratitude to Yamato Takeru for his care during a previous ordeal. Yamato Takeru’s lament, “Azuma haya” (“My wife!”), upon reaching Azuma, is said to have named the eastern provinces “Azuma,” a geonymic tale.宇治谷孟 『日本書紀（上）全現代語訳』 169ページ次田真幸 『古事記（中）全訳注』 153ページ == Hitachi no Kuni Fudoki == In the {{ill|Hitachi no Kuni Fudoki|ja|常陸国風土記}}, Ototachibana-hime appears as Ōtachibana-hime or Empress Tachibana, while Yamato Takeru is referred to as Emperor Yamato.植垣節也 『風土記 新編日本古典文学全集5』 388ページ == Sendaikujihongi == In the {{ill|Sendaikujihongi|ja|先代旧事本紀}}, Ototachibana-hime is recorded as the mother of Wakatakehiko-no-Mikoto and eight other sons.植垣節也 『風土記 新編日本古典文学全集5』 417ページ == Shrines and Legends == Ototachibana-hime’s legend is central to shrines across Kazusa (modern-day Chiba) and beyond. The sacred tachibana tree associated with her suggests she may have been a shaman.小野一之・鈴木彰・谷口榮・樋口州男 『人物伝小辞典 古代・中世編』 54ページ == References == == Bibliography == * 宇治谷孟 『日本書紀（上）全現代語訳』 講談社、1988年 ISBN 4-06-158833-8 * 次田真幸 『古事記（中）全訳注』 講談社、1980年 ISBN 4-06-158208-9 * 植垣節也 『風土記 新編日本古典文学全集5』 小学館、1997年 ISBN 4-09-658005-8 * 菊池利夫 『東京湾史』 大日本図書印刷 1974年 * 小野一之・鈴木彰・谷口榮・樋口州男 『人物伝小辞典 古代・中世編』 東京堂出版 2004年 == Related Topics == * {{ill|Azuma|ja|東#呼称}} * {{ill|Tamasaki Shrine|ja|玉崎神社}} * {{ill|Ane Saki Shrine|ja|姉埼神社}} * {{ill|Soga-hime Shrine|ja|蘇我比咩神社}} * {{ill|Kimisara Tower|ja|きみさらずタワー}} {{Japan mythology}} [[Category:Ototachibana-hime]] [[Category:Women in ancient Japan]] [[Category:Consorts of Yamato Takeru]] [[Category:Legendary Japanese women]] [[Category:Japanese deities (human-related)]] [[Category:Birth and death dates unknown]] [[Category:ZZZZZZZZZ Unlinked]][[category:unsorted completion categories]] [[Category:kk]] [[Category:kk]][[category:kk]] [[Category:attempting to force efresh]] [[Category:bump-01-18]] [[ja:弟橘媛]]

[[File:Ototachibanahime.png|thumb|Ototachibanahime (from ''{{ill|Zenkengojitsu|ja|前賢故実}}'')]] '''Ototachibanahime''' (弟橘媛, birth unknown – 40th year of Emperor Keikō) was, according to the ''{{ill|Nihon Shoki|ja|日本書紀}}'', the consort of {{ill|Yamato Takeru|ja|ヤマトタケル}}. In the ''{{ill|Kojiki|ja|古事記}}'', she is referred to as Otachibanahime no Mikoto, the consort of Yamato Takeru. == Ototachibanahime in the Chronicles == According to the ''Nihon Shoki'', she was the daughter of {{ill|Takeoshi Yama Tarine|ja|建忍山垂根}} of the {{ill|Hozumi clan|ja|穂積氏}} and bore a son, {{ill|Prince Wakatarashihiko|ja|稚武彦王}}, with Yamato Takeru.''Nihon Shoki'' (translated by {{ill|Mitsuo Ueda|ja|宇治谷孟}}), p. 174 {{Quotation|...When they reached Sagami and intended to cross to Kazusa, Yamato Takeru declared, “This small sea can be easily leapt over.” However, halfway through the journey, a violent storm arose, and the ship began to drift. One of the consorts accompanying him, named Ototachibanahime, daughter of Takeoshi Yama Tarine, addressed him, saying, “This storm must be the work of the sea deity. I will take your place and appease the gods.” With those words, she leapt into the sea. The storm subsided immediately, and the ship safely reached shore. The place was later called ''Tachimizu'' in her honor...''Nihon Shoki'' (translated by Mitsuo Ueda), p. 168}} The {{ill|Tachibana Shrine (Mobara)|ja|橘樹神社 (茂原市)}} in {{ill|Mobara|ja|茂原市}}, Chiba Prefecture, is said to be where Yamato Takeru commemorated Ototachibanahime by planting a tachibana (citrus) tree as her grave marker. This shrine is a significant site, being listed in the ''Engishiki'' as one of the principal shrines of Kazusa Province and the only shrine officially dedicated to Ototachibanahime. == ''Kojiki'' Narrative == The ''Kojiki'' recounts a similar story of Ototachibanahime's sacrifice during Yamato Takeru's journey. When a storm arose in the [[Uraga Channel]], she offered herself to calm the sea: {{Quotation|...Then, as they attempted to cross the sea at Hashirimizu, a storm arose. To appease the wrath of the sea deity, Ototachibanahime declared, “I will take my lord’s place to calm the sea and ensure the success of the mission.” She laid eight layers of sedge mats, skin mats, and silk mats on the waves, seated herself on them, and entered the sea. The waves subsided, allowing the ship to proceed.''Kojiki'' (translated by {{ill|Makoto Tsuda|ja|次田真幸}}), p. 150}} Her parting song, only found in the ''Kojiki,'' reflects her gratitude towards Yamato Takeru for his concern during their peril: * ''As the fire blazes on Saho's field, you asked of me amidst the flames. My lord, how kind you are!''''Kojiki'' (translated by Makoto Tsuda), p. 147 == ''Hitachi Fudoki'' Account == In the ''{{ill|Hitachi Fudoki|ja|常陸国風土記}}'', Ototachibanahime appears as ''Otachibanahime no Mikoto'' or ''Tachibana Kōgō'' (Empress Tachibana). Yamato Takeru, referred to as Emperor Yamato Takeru, is depicted reuniting with her in the area now known as Itako and Namegata in Ibaraki Prefecture.''Fudoki,'' {{ill|New Japanese Classic Literature Complete Works|ja|新編日本古典文学全集}}, p. 388 == Shrines and Legends == Shrines dedicated to Ototachibanahime, such as {{ill|Tachibana Shrine (Mobara)|ja|橘樹神社 (茂原市)}} and {{ill|Hashirimizu Shrine|ja|走水神社}}, are located in Chiba Prefecture and other regions. Legends surrounding her, including the origin of place names like ''Sodegaura'' in Chiba and Narashino, testify to her lasting cultural significance. == References == {{Reflist}} == Bibliography == * Mitsuo Ueda, ''Nihon Shoki (Volume I): Full Modern Translation,'' Kodansha, 1988. ISBN 4-06-158833-8 * Makoto Tsuda, ''Kojiki (Volume II): Full Annotated Translation,'' Kodansha, 1980. ISBN 4-06-158208-9 * Toshiwo Kikuchi, ''History of Tokyo Bay,'' Dai-Nihon Printing, 1974 * Ichiyuki Ono et al., ''Biographical Dictionary of Ancient and Medieval Japan,'' Tokyo-do Publishing, 2004. == See Also == * {{ill|Azuma|ja|東#呼称}} * {{ill|Tamasaki Shrine|ja|玉崎神社}} * {{ill|Ane Saki Shrine|ja|姉埼神社}} * {{ill|Soga Hime Shrine|ja|蘇我比咩神社}} * {{ill|Kisarazu Tower|ja|きみさらずタワー}} [[Category:ZZZZZZZZZ completion categories]] [[Category:kk]] [[Category:Unknown birth and death years]] [[ja:弟橘媛]] '''Ototachibana-hime''' (弟橘媛) was the wife of {{ill|Yamato Takeru|ja|ヤマトタケル}}, according to the {{ill|Nihon Shoki|ja|日本書紀}}. In the {{ill|Kojiki|ja|古事記}}, she is referred to as Otachibana-hime no Mikoto (弟橘比売命), the consort of Yamato Takeru. == Ototachibana-hime in the Kojiki and Nihon Shoki == In the {{ill|Nihon Shoki|ja|日本書紀}}, she is described as the daughter of {{ill|Oshiyama-no-Sukune|ja|忍山宿禰}}, an ancestor of the {{ill|Hozumi clan|ja|穂積氏}}. She bore Yamato Takeru a son, {{ill|Wakatakehiko-no-Mikoto|ja|稚武彦王}}.宇治谷孟 『日本書紀（上）全現代語訳』 174ページ {{Quotation|While crossing the sea from Sagami to Kazusa, Yamato Takeru boasted, “This small sea, I could leap over it.” However, a violent storm arose, and the ship began to drift. Ototachibana-hime, his consort, said, “This storm must be the work of the sea god. Allow me to enter the sea as your substitute.” With these words, she entered the waves, and the storm ceased. The ship safely reached the shore, and the sea was named Tachibana Sea in her honor.宇治谷孟 『日本書紀（上）全現代語訳』 168ページ}} In {{ill|Mobara, Chiba|ja|茂原市}}, {{ill|Tachibana Shrine (Mobara)|ja|橘樹神社}} honors Ototachibana-hime. The shrine is an {{ill|Engishiki|ja|延喜式}}-listed shrine, one of Kazusa's five major shrines, and the only shrine recorded in historical texts to enshrine her. According to legend, Yamato Takeru planted a tachibana tree as her grave marker here.菊池利夫 『東京湾史』 80ページ In the {{ill|Kojiki|ja|古事記}}, Ototachibana-hime offered herself to calm the sea gods’ wrath as Yamato Takeru encountered a storm at Hashirimizu. She placed eight layers of sedge, hide, and silk mats on the waves, sat on them, and entered the sea, calming the waters.次田真幸 『古事記（中）全訳注』 150ページ {{Quotation|She said, “I shall enter the sea as your substitute. Please proceed safely with your campaign.” She then spread eight layers of sedge, hide, and silk mats over the waves, seated herself on them, and entered the sea. The storm subsided, and the ship advanced. As she descended, she sang:
* “In Aibu’s fields, flames rise; standing amid them, you questioned me, my lord.”
Her comb was found washed ashore seven days later, and Yamato Takeru enshrined it, creating her tomb.次田真幸 『古事記（中）全訳注』 147ページ}} The comb is said to be enshrined at Tachibana Shrine, and her poem expresses her gratitude to Yamato Takeru for his care during a previous ordeal. Yamato Takeru’s lament, “Azuma haya” (“My wife!”), upon reaching Azuma, is said to have named the eastern provinces “Azuma,” a geonymic tale.宇治谷孟 『日本書紀（上）全現代語訳』 169ページ次田真幸 『古事記（中）全訳注』 153ページ == Hitachi no Kuni Fudoki == In the {{ill|Hitachi no Kuni Fudoki|ja|常陸国風土記}}, Ototachibana-hime appears as Ōtachibana-hime or Empress Tachibana, while Yamato Takeru is referred to as Emperor Yamato.植垣節也 『風土記 新編日本古典文学全集5』 388ページ == Sendaikujihongi == In the {{ill|Sendaikujihongi|ja|先代旧事本紀}}, Ototachibana-hime is recorded as the mother of Wakatakehiko-no-Mikoto and eight other sons.植垣節也 『風土記 新編日本古典文学全集5』 417ページ == Shrines and Legends == Ototachibana-hime’s legend is central to shrines across Kazusa (modern-day Chiba) and beyond. Shrines such as {{ill|Tachibana Shrine (Kanagawa)|ja|橘樹神社}}, {{ill|Nomura Shrine|ja|能褒野神社}}, and {{ill|Hashirimizu Shrine|ja|走水神社 (横須賀市)}} enshrine her alongside Yamato Takeru. Places like {{ill|Sodegaura|ja|袖ケ浦}} are named after the legend of her sleeves washing ashore. The sacred tachibana tree associated with her suggests she may have been a shaman.小野一之・鈴木彰・谷口榮・樋口州男 『人物伝小辞典 古代・中世編』 54ページ == References == == Bibliography == * 宇治谷孟 『日本書紀（上）全現代語訳』 講談社、1988年 ISBN 4-06-158833-8 * 次田真幸 『古事記（中）全訳注』 講談社、1980年 ISBN 4-06-158208-9 * 植垣節也 『風土記 新編日本古典文学全集5』 小学館、1997年 ISBN 4-09-658005-8 * 菊池利夫 『東京湾史』 大日本図書印刷 1974年 * 小野一之・鈴木彰・谷口榮・樋口州男 『人物伝小辞典 古代・中世編』 東京堂出版 2004年 == Related Topics == * {{ill|Azuma|ja|東#呼称}} * {{ill|Tamasaki Shrine|ja|玉崎神社}} * {{ill|Ane Saki Shrine|ja|姉埼神社}} * {{ill|Soga-hime Shrine|ja|蘇我比咩神社}} * {{ill|Kimisara Tower|ja|きみさらずタワー}} {{Japan mythology}} [[Category:Ototachibana-hime]] [[Category:Women in ancient Japan]] [[Category:Consorts of Yamato Takeru]] [[Category:Legendary Japanese women]] [[Category:Japanese deities (human-related)]] [[Category:Birth and death dates unknown]] [[Category:ZZZZZZZZZ Unlinked]][[category:unsorted completion categories]] [[Category:kk]] [[Category:kk]][[category:kk]] [[Category:attempting to force efresh]] [[Category:bump-01-18]] [[Category:bump 1-20]] [[ja:弟橘媛]]

My lord, how kind you are!''''Kojiki'' (translated by Makoto Tsuda), p. 147 == ''Hitachi Fudoki'' Account == In the ''{{ill|Hitachi Fudoki|ja|常陸国風土記}}'', Ototachibanahime appears as ''Otachibanahime no Mikoto'' or ''Tachibana Kōgō'' (Empress Tachibana). Yamato Takeru, referred to as Emperor Yamato Takeru, is depicted reuniting with her in the area now known as Itako and Namegata in Ibaraki Prefecture.''Fudoki,'' {{ill|New Japanese Classic Literature Complete Works|ja|新編日本古典文学全集}}, p. 388 == Shrines and Legends == Shrines dedicated to Ototachibanahime, such as {{ill|Tachibana Shrine (Mobara)|ja|橘樹神社 (茂原市)}} and {{ill|Hashirimizu Shrine|ja|走水神社}}, are located in Chiba Prefecture and other regions. Legends surrounding her, including the origin of place names like ''Sodegaura'' in Chiba and Narashino, testify to her lasting cultural significance. == References == {{Reflist}} == Bibliography == * Mitsuo Ueda, ''Nihon Shoki (Volume I): Full Modern Translation,'' Kodansha, 1988. ISBN 4-06-158833-8 * Makoto Tsuda, ''Kojiki (Volume II): Full Annotated Translation,'' Kodansha, 1980. Her comb was found washed ashore seven days later, and Yamato Takeru enshrined it, creating her tomb.次田真幸 『古事記（中）全訳注』 147ページ}} The comb is said to be enshrined at Tachibana Shrine, and her poem expresses her gratitude to Yamato Takeru for his care during a previous ordeal. Yamato Takeru’s lament, “Azuma haya” (“My wife!”), upon reaching Azuma, is said to have named the eastern provinces “Azuma,” a geonymic tale.宇治谷孟 『日本書紀（上）全現代語訳』 169ページ次田真幸 『古事記（中）全訳注』 153ページ == Hitachi no Kuni Fudoki == In the {{ill|Hitachi no Kuni Fudoki|ja|常陸国風土記}}, Ototachibana-hime appears as Ōtachibana-hime or Empress Tachibana, while Yamato Takeru is referred to as Emperor Yamato.植垣節也 『風土記 新編日本古典文学全集5』 388ページ == Sendaikujihongi == In the {{ill|Sendaikujihongi|ja|先代旧事本紀}}, Ototachibana-hime is recorded as the mother of Wakatakehiko-no-Mikoto and eight other sons.植垣節也 『風土記 新編日本古典文学全集5』 417ページ == Shrines and Legends == Ototachibana-hime’s legend is central to shrines across Kazusa (modern-day Chiba) and beyond. Shrines such as {{ill|Tachibana Shrine (Kanagawa)|ja|橘樹神社}}, {{ill|Nomura Shrine|ja|能褒野神社}}, and {{ill|Hashirimizu Shrine|ja|走水神社 (横須賀市)}} enshrine her alongside Yamato Takeru. Places like {{ill|Sodegaura|ja|袖ケ浦}} are named after the legend of her sleeves washing ashore. The sacred tachibana tree associated with her suggests she may have been a shaman.小野一之・鈴木彰・谷口榮・樋口州男 『人物伝小辞典 古代・中世編』 54ページ == References == == Bibliography == * 宇治谷孟 『日本書紀（上）全現代語訳』 講談社、1988年 ISBN 4-06-158833-8 * 次田真幸 『古事記（中）全訳注』 講談社、1980年 ISBN 4-06-158208-9 * 植垣節也 『風土記 新編日本古典文学全集5』 小学館、1997年 ISBN 4-09-658005-8 * 菊池利夫 『東京湾史』 大日本図書印刷 1974年 * 小野一之・鈴木彰・谷口榮・樋口州男 『人物伝小辞典 古代・中世編』 東京堂出版 2004年 == Related Topics == * {{ill|Azuma|ja|東#呼称}} * {{ill|Tamasaki Shrine|ja|玉崎神社}} * {{ill|Ane Saki Shrine|ja|姉埼神社}} * {{ill|Soga-hime Shrine|ja|蘇我比咩神社}} * {{ill|Kimisara Tower|ja|きみさらずタワー}} {{Japan mythology}} [[Category:Ototachibana-hime]] [[Category:Women in ancient Japan]] [[Category:Consorts of Yamato Takeru]] [[Category:Legendary Japanese women]] [[Category:Japanese deities (human-related)]] [[Category:Birth and death dates unknown]] [[Category:ZZZZZZZZZ Unlinked]][[category:unsorted completion categories]] [[Category:kk]] [[Category:kk]][[category:kk]] [[Category:attempting to force efresh]] [[Category:bump-01-18]] [[ja:弟橘媛]]

[[File:Ototachibanahime.png|thumb|Ototachibanahime (from ''{{ill|Zenkengojitsu|ja|前賢故実}}'')]] '''Ototachibanahime''' (弟橘媛, birth unknown – 40th year of Emperor Keikō) was, according to the ''{{ill|Nihon Shoki|ja|日本書紀}}'', the consort of {{ill|Yamato Takeru|ja|ヤマトタケル}}. In the ''{{ill|Kojiki|ja|古事記}}'', she is referred to as Otachibanahime no Mikoto, the consort of Yamato Takeru. == Ototachibanahime in the Chronicles == According to the ''Nihon Shoki'', she was the daughter of {{ill|Takeoshi Yama Tarine|ja|建忍山垂根}} of the {{ill|Hozumi clan|ja|穂積氏}} and bore a son, {{ill|Prince Wakatarashihiko|ja|稚武彦王}}, with Yamato Takeru.''Nihon Shoki'' (translated by {{ill|Mitsuo Ueda|ja|宇治谷孟}}), p. 174 {{Quotation|...When they reached Sagami and intended to cross to Kazusa, Yamato Takeru declared, “This small sea can be easily leapt over.” However, halfway through the journey, a violent storm arose, and the ship began to drift. One of the consorts accompanying him, named Ototachibanahime, daughter of Takeoshi Yama Tarine, addressed him, saying, “This storm must be the work of the sea deity. I will take your place and appease the gods.” With those words, she leapt into the sea. The storm subsided immediately, and the ship safely reached shore. The place was later called ''Tachimizu'' in her honor...''Nihon Shoki'' (translated by Mitsuo Ueda), p. 168}} The {{ill|Tachibana Shrine (Mobara)|ja|橘樹神社 (茂原市)}} in {{ill|Mobara|ja|茂原市}}, Chiba Prefecture, is said to be where Yamato Takeru commemorated Ototachibanahime by planting a tachibana (citrus) tree as her grave marker. This shrine is a significant site, being listed in the ''Engishiki'' as one of the principal shrines of Kazusa Province and the only shrine officially dedicated to Ototachibanahime. == ''Kojiki'' Narrative == The ''Kojiki'' recounts a similar story of Ototachibanahime's sacrifice during Yamato Takeru's journey. When a storm arose in the [[Uraga Channel]], she offered herself to calm the sea: {{Quotation|...Then, as they attempted to cross the sea at Hashirimizu, a storm arose. To appease the wrath of the sea deity, Ototachibanahime declared, “I will take my lord’s place to calm the sea and ensure the success of the mission.” She laid eight layers of sedge mats, skin mats, and silk mats on the waves, seated herself on them, and entered the sea. The waves subsided, allowing the ship to proceed.''Kojiki'' (translated by {{ill|Makoto Tsuda|ja|次田真幸}}), p. 150}} Her parting song, only found in the ''Kojiki,'' reflects her gratitude towards Yamato Takeru for his concern during their peril: * ''As the fire blazes on Saho's field, you asked of me amidst the flames. My lord, how kind you are!''''Kojiki'' (translated by Makoto Tsuda), p. 147 == ''Hitachi Fudoki'' Account == In the ''{{ill|Hitachi Fudoki|ja|常陸国風土記}}'', Ototachibanahime appears as ''Otachibanahime no Mikoto'' or ''Tachibana Kōgō'' (Empress Tachibana). Yamato Takeru, referred to as Emperor Yamato Takeru, is depicted reuniting with her in the area now known as Itako and Namegata in Ibaraki Prefecture.''Fudoki,'' {{ill|New Japanese Classic Literature Complete Works|ja|新編日本古典文学全集}}, p. 388 == Shrines and Legends == Shrines dedicated to Ototachibanahime, such as {{ill|Tachibana Shrine (Mobara)|ja|橘樹神社 (茂原市)}} and {{ill|Hashirimizu Shrine|ja|走水神社}}, are located in Chiba Prefecture and other regions. Legends surrounding her, including the origin of place names like ''Sodegaura'' in Chiba and Narashino, testify to her lasting cultural significance. == References == {{Reflist}} == Bibliography == * Mitsuo Ueda, ''Nihon Shoki (Volume I): Full Modern Translation,'' Kodansha, 1988. ISBN 4-06-158833-8 * Makoto Tsuda, ''Kojiki (Volume II): Full Annotated Translation,'' Kodansha, 1980. ISBN 4-06-158208-9 * Toshiwo Kikuchi, ''History of Tokyo Bay,'' Dai-Nihon Printing, 1974 * Ichiyuki Ono et al., ''Biographical Dictionary of Ancient and Medieval Japan,'' Tokyo-do Publishing, 2004. == See Also == * {{ill|Azuma|ja|東#呼称}} * {{ill|Tamasaki Shrine|ja|玉崎神社}} * {{ill|Ane Saki Shrine|ja|姉埼神社}} * {{ill|Soga Hime Shrine|ja|蘇我比咩神社}} * {{ill|Kisarazu Tower|ja|きみさらずタワー}} [[Category:ZZZZZZZZZ '''Ototachibana-hime''' (弟橘媛) was the wife of {{ill|Yamato Takeru|ja|ヤマトタケル}}, according to the {{ill|Nihon Shoki|ja|日本書紀}}. In the {{ill|Kojiki|ja|古事記}}, she is referred to as Otachibana-hime no Mikoto (弟橘比売命), the consort of Yamato Takeru. == Ototachibana-hime in the Kojiki and Nihon Shoki == In the {{ill|Nihon Shoki|ja|日本書紀}}, she is described as the daughter of {{ill|Oshiyama-no-Sukune|ja|忍山宿禰}}, an ancestor of the {{ill|Hozumi clan|ja|穂積氏}}. She bore Yamato Takeru a son, {{ill|Wakatakehiko-no-Mikoto|ja|稚武彦王}}.宇治谷孟 『日本書紀（上）全現代語訳』 174ページ {{Quotation|While crossing the sea from Sagami to Kazusa, Yamato Takeru boasted, “This small sea, I could leap over it.” However, a violent storm arose, and the ship began to drift. Ototachibana-hime, his consort, said, “This storm must be the work of the sea god. Allow me to enter the sea as your substitute.” With these words, she entered the waves, and the storm ceased. The ship safely reached the shore, and the sea was named Tachibana Sea in her honor.宇治谷孟 『日本書紀（上）全現代語訳』 168ページ}} In {{ill|Mobara, Chiba|ja|茂原市}}, {{ill|Tachibana Shrine (Mobara)|ja|橘樹神社}} honors Ototachibana-hime. The shrine is an {{ill|Engishiki|ja|延喜式}}-listed shrine, one of Kazusa's five major shrines, and the only shrine recorded in historical texts to enshrine her. According to legend, Yamato Takeru planted a tachibana tree as her grave marker here.菊池利夫 『東京湾史』 80ページ In the {{ill|Kojiki|ja|古事記}}, Ototachibana-hime offered herself to calm the sea gods’ wrath as Yamato Takeru encountered a storm at Hashirimizu. She placed eight layers of sedge, hide, and silk mats on the waves, sat on them, and entered the sea, calming the waters.次田真幸 『古事記（中）全訳注』 150ページ {{Quotation|She said, “I shall enter the sea as your substitute. Please proceed safely with your campaign.” She then spread eight layers of sedge, hide, and silk mats over the waves, seated herself on them, and entered the sea. The storm subsided, and the ship advanced. As she descended, she sang:
* “In Aibu’s fields, flames rise; standing amid them, you questioned me, my lord.”
Her comb was found washed ashore seven days later, and Yamato Takeru enshrined it, creating her tomb.次田真幸 『古事記（中）全訳注』 147ページ}} The comb is said to be enshrined at Tachibana Shrine, and her poem expresses her gratitude to Yamato Takeru for his care during a previous ordeal. Yamato Takeru’s lament, “Azuma haya” (“My wife!”), upon reaching Azuma, is said to have named the eastern provinces “Azuma,” a geonymic tale.宇治谷孟 『日本書紀（上）全現代語訳』 169ページ次田真幸 『古事記（中）全訳注』 153ページ == Hitachi no Kuni Fudoki == In the {{ill|Hitachi no Kuni Fudoki|ja|常陸国風土記}}, Ototachibana-hime appears as Ōtachibana-hime or Empress Tachibana, while Yamato Takeru is referred to as Emperor Yamato.植垣節也 『風土記 新編日本古典文学全集5』 388ページ == Sendaikujihongi == In the {{ill|Sendaikujihongi|ja|先代旧事本紀}}, Ototachibana-hime is recorded as the mother of Wakatakehiko-no-Mikoto and eight other sons.植垣節也 『風土記 新編日本古典文学全集5』 417ページ == Shrines and Legends == Ototachibana-hime’s legend is central to shrines across Kazusa (modern-day Chiba) and beyond. Shrines such as {{ill|Tachibana Shrine (Kanagawa)|ja|橘樹神社}}, {{ill|Nomura Shrine|ja|能褒野神社}}, and {{ill|Hashirimizu Shrine|ja|走水神社 (横須賀市)}} enshrine her alongside Yamato Takeru. Places like {{ill|Sodegaura|ja|袖ケ浦}} are named after the legend of her sleeves washing ashore. The sacred tachibana tree associated with her suggests she may have been a shaman.小野一之・鈴木彰・谷口榮・樋口州男 『人物伝小辞典 古代・中世編』 54ページ == References == == Bibliography == * 宇治谷孟 『日本書紀（上）全現代語訳』 講談社、1988年 ISBN 4-06-158833-8 * 次田真幸 『古事記（中）全訳注』 講談社、1980年 ISBN 4-06-158208-9 * 植垣節也 『風土記 新編日本古典文学全集5』 小学館、1997年 ISBN 4-09-658005-8 * 菊池利夫 『東京湾史』 大日本図書印刷 1974年 * 小野一之・鈴木彰・谷口榮・樋口州男 『人物伝小辞典 古代・中世編』 東京堂出版 2004年 == Related Topics == * {{ill|Azuma|ja|東#呼称}} * {{ill|Tamasaki Shrine|ja|玉崎神社}} * {{ill|Ane Saki Shrine|ja|姉埼神社}} * {{ill|Soga-hime Shrine|ja|蘇我比咩神社}} * {{ill|Kimisara Tower|ja|きみさらずタワー}} {{Japan mythology}} [[Category:Ototachibana-hime]] [[Category:Women in ancient Japan]] [[Category:Consorts of Yamato Takeru]] [[Category:Legendary Japanese women]] [[Category:Japanese deities (human-related)]] [[Category:Birth and death dates unknown]] [[Category:ZZZZZZZZZ Unlinked]][[category:unsorted completion categories]] [[Category:attempting to force efresh]] [[Category:bump-01-18]] [[ja:弟橘媛]]

She bore Yamato Takeru a son, {{ill|Wakatakehiko-no-Mikoto|ja|稚武彦王}}.宇治谷孟 『日本書紀（上）全現代語訳』 174ページ {{Quotation|While crossing the sea from Sagami to Kazusa, Yamato Takeru boasted, “This small sea, I could leap over it.” However, a violent storm arose, and the ship began to drift. Ototachibana-hime, his consort, said, “This storm must be the work of the sea god. Allow me to enter the sea as your substitute.” With these words, she entered the waves, and the storm ceased. The ship safely reached the shore, and the sea was named Tachibana Sea in her honor.宇治谷孟 『日本書紀（上）全現代語訳』 168ページ}} In {{ill|Mobara, Chiba|ja|茂原市}}, {{ill|Tachibana Shrine (Mobara)|ja|橘樹神社}} honors Ototachibana-hime. The shrine is an {{ill|Engishiki|ja|延喜式}}-listed shrine, one of Kazusa's five major shrines, and the only shrine recorded in historical texts to enshrine her. According to legend, Yamato Takeru planted a tachibana tree as her grave marker here.菊池利夫 『東京湾史』 80ページ In the {{ill|Kojiki|ja|古事記}}, Ototachibana-hime offered herself to calm the sea gods’ wrath as Yamato Takeru encountered a storm at Hashirimizu. She placed eight layers of sedge, hide, and silk mats on the waves, sat on them, and entered the sea, calming the waters.次田真幸 『古事記（中）全訳注』 150ページ {{Quotation|She said, “I shall enter the sea as your substitute. Please proceed safely with your campaign.” She then spread eight layers of sedge, hide, and silk mats over the waves, seated herself on them, and entered the sea. The storm subsided, and the ship advanced. As she descended, she sang:
* “In Aibu’s fields, flames rise; standing amid them, you questioned me, my lord.”
Her comb was found washed ashore seven days later, and Yamato Takeru enshrined it, creating her tomb.次田真幸 『古事記（中）全訳注』 147ページ}} The comb is said to be enshrined at Tachibana Shrine, and her poem expresses her gratitude to Yamato Takeru for his care during a previous ordeal. Yamato Takeru’s lament, “Azuma haya” (“My wife!”), upon reaching Azuma, is said to have named the eastern provinces “Azuma,” a geonymic tale.宇治谷孟 『日本書紀（上）全現代語訳』 169ページ次田真幸 『古事記（中）全訳注』 153ページ == Hitachi no Kuni Fudoki == In the {{ill|Hitachi no Kuni Fudoki|ja|常陸国風土記}}, Ototachibana-hime appears as Ōtachibana-hime or Empress Tachibana, while Yamato Takeru is referred to as Emperor Yamato.植垣節也 『風土記 新編日本古典文学全集5』 388ページ == Sendaikujihongi == In the {{ill|Sendaikujihongi|ja|先代旧事本紀}}, Ototachibana-hime is recorded as the mother of Wakatakehiko-no-Mikoto and eight other sons.植垣節也 『風土記 新編日本古典文学全集5』 417ページ == Shrines and Legends == Ototachibana-hime’s legend is central to shrines across Kazusa (modern-day Chiba) and beyond. Shrines such as {{ill|Tachibana Shrine (Kanagawa)|ja|橘樹神社}}, {{ill|Nomura Shrine|ja|能褒野神社}}, and {{ill|Hashirimizu Shrine|ja|走水神社 (横須賀市)}} enshrine her alongside Yamato Takeru. Places like {{ill|Sodegaura|ja|袖ケ浦}} are named after the legend of her sleeves washing ashore. The sacred tachibana tree associated with her suggests she may have been a shaman.小野一之・鈴木彰・谷口榮・樋口州男 『人物伝小辞典 古代・中世編』 54ページ == References == == Bibliography == * 宇治谷孟 『日本書紀（上）全現代語訳』 講談社、1988年 ISBN 4-06-158833-8 * 次田真幸 『古事記（中）全訳注』 講談社、1980年 ISBN 4-06-158208-9 * 植垣節也 『風土記 新編日本古典文学全集5』 小学館、1997年 ISBN 4-09-658005-8 * 菊池利夫 『東京湾史』 大日本図書印刷 1974年 * 小野一之・鈴木彰・谷口榮・樋口州男 『人物伝小辞典 古代・中世編』 東京堂出版 2004年 == Related Topics == * {{ill|Azuma|ja|東#呼称}} * {{ill|Tamasaki Shrine|ja|玉崎神社}} * {{ill|Ane Saki Shrine|ja|姉埼神社}} * {{ill|Soga-hime Shrine|ja|蘇我比咩神社}} * {{ill|Kimisara Tower|ja|きみさらずタワー}} {{Japan mythology}} [[Category:Ototachibana-hime]] [[Category:Women in ancient Japan]] [[Category:Consorts of Yamato Takeru]] [[Category:Legendary Japanese women]] [[Category:Japanese deities (human-related)]] [[Category:Birth and death dates unknown]] [[Category:ZZZZZZZZZ Unlinked]][[category:unsorted completion categories]] [[Category:attempting to force efresh]] [[Category:bump-01-18]] [[ja:弟橘媛]]

[[File:Ototachibanahime.png|thumb|Ototachibanahime (from ''{{ill|Zenkengojitsu|ja|前賢故実}}'')]] '''Ototachibanahime''' (弟橘媛, birth unknown – 40th year of Emperor Keikō) was, according to the ''{{ill|Nihon Shoki|ja|日本書紀}}'', the consort of {{ill|Yamato Takeru|ja|ヤマトタケル}}. In the ''{{ill|Kojiki|ja|古事記}}'', she is referred to as Otachibanahime no Mikoto, the consort of Yamato Takeru. == Ototachibanahime in the Chronicles == According to the ''Nihon Shoki'', she was the daughter of {{ill|Takeoshi Yama Tarine|ja|建忍山垂根}} of the {{ill|Hozumi clan|ja|穂積氏}} and bore a son, {{ill|Prince Wakatarashihiko|ja|稚武彦王}}, with Yamato Takeru.''Nihon Shoki'' (translated by {{ill|Mitsuo Ueda|ja|宇治谷孟}}), p. 174 {{Quotation|...When they reached Sagami and intended to cross to Kazusa, Yamato Takeru declared, “This small sea can be easily leapt over.” However, halfway through the journey, a violent storm arose, and the ship began to drift. One of the consorts accompanying him, named Ototachibanahime, daughter of Takeoshi Yama Tarine, addressed him, saying, “This storm must be the work of the sea deity. I will take your place and appease the gods.” With those words, she leapt into the sea. The storm subsided immediately, and the ship safely reached shore. The place was later called ''Tachimizu'' in her honor...''Nihon Shoki'' (translated by Mitsuo Ueda), p. 168}} The {{ill|Tachibana Shrine (Mobara)|ja|橘樹神社 (茂原市)}} in {{ill|Mobara|ja|茂原市}}, Chiba Prefecture, is said to be where Yamato Takeru commemorated Ototachibanahime by planting a tachibana (citrus) tree as her grave marker. This shrine is a significant site, being listed in the ''Engishiki'' as one of the principal shrines of Kazusa Province and the only shrine officially dedicated to Ototachibanahime. == ''Kojiki'' Narrative == The ''Kojiki'' recounts a similar story of Ototachibanahime's sacrifice during Yamato Takeru's journey. When a storm arose in the [[Uraga Channel]], she offered herself to calm the sea: {{Quotation|...Then, as they attempted to cross the sea at Hashirimizu, a storm arose. To appease the wrath of the sea deity, Ototachibanahime declared, “I will take my lord’s place to calm the sea and ensure the success of the mission.” She laid eight layers of sedge mats, skin mats, and silk mats on the waves, seated herself on them, and entered the sea. The waves subsided, allowing the ship to proceed.''Kojiki'' (translated by {{ill|Makoto Tsuda|ja|次田真幸}}), p. 150}} Her parting song, only found in the ''Kojiki,'' reflects her gratitude towards Yamato Takeru for his concern during their peril: * ''As the fire blazes on Saho's field, you asked of me amidst the flames. My lord, how kind you are!''''Kojiki'' (translated by Makoto Tsuda), p. 147 == ''Hitachi Fudoki'' Account == In the ''{{ill|Hitachi Fudoki|ja|常陸国風土記}}'', Ototachibanahime appears as ''Otachibanahime no Mikoto'' or ''Tachibana Kōgō'' (Empress Tachibana). Yamato Takeru, referred to as Emperor Yamato Takeru, is depicted reuniting with her in the area now known as Itako and Namegata in Ibaraki Prefecture.''Fudoki,'' {{ill|New Japanese Classic Literature Complete Works|ja|新編日本古典文学全集}}, p. 388 == Shrines and Legends == Shrines dedicated to Ototachibanahime, such as {{ill|Tachibana Shrine (Mobara)|ja|橘樹神社 (茂原市)}} and {{ill|Hashirimizu Shrine|ja|走水神社}}, are located in Chiba Prefecture and other regions. Legends surrounding her, including the origin of place names like ''Sodegaura'' in Chiba and Narashino, testify to her lasting cultural significance. == References == {{Reflist}} == Bibliography == * Mitsuo Ueda, ''Nihon Shoki (Volume I): Full Modern Translation,'' Kodansha, 1988. ISBN 4-06-158833-8 * Makoto Tsuda, ''Kojiki (Volume II): Full Annotated Translation,'' Kodansha, 1980. ISBN 4-06-158208-9 * Toshiwo Kikuchi, ''History of Tokyo Bay,'' Dai-Nihon Printing, 1974 * Ichiyuki Ono et al., ''Biographical Dictionary of Ancient and Medieval Japan,'' Tokyo-do Publishing, 2004. == See Also == * {{ill|Azuma|ja|東#呼称}} * {{ill|Tamasaki Shrine|ja|玉崎神社}} * {{ill|Ane Saki Shrine|ja|姉埼神社}} * {{ill|Soga Hime Shrine|ja|蘇我比咩神社}} * {{ill|Kisarazu Tower|ja|きみさらずタワー}} [[Category:ZZZZZZZZZ Unlinked]] [[Category:pages needing attribution]][[Category:Ototachibana-hime]][[Category:pages needing attribution]] [[category:unsorted completion categories]] [[Category:ZZZZZZZZZ Unlinked]] '''Ototachibana-hime''' (弟橘媛) was the wife of {{ill|Yamato Takeru|ja|ヤマトタケル}}, according to the {{ill|Nihon Shoki|ja|日本書紀}}. In the {{ill|Kojiki|ja|古事記}}, she is referred to as Otachibana-hime no Mikoto (弟橘比売命), the consort of Yamato Takeru. == Ototachibana-hime in the Kojiki and Nihon Shoki == In the {{ill|Nihon Shoki|ja|日本書紀}}, she is described as the daughter of {{ill|Oshiyama-no-Sukune|ja|忍山宿禰}}, an ancestor of the {{ill|Hozumi clan|ja|穂積氏}}. She bore Yamato Takeru a son, {{ill|Wakatakehiko-no-Mikoto|ja|稚武彦王}}.宇治谷孟 『日本書紀（上）全現代語訳』 174ページ {{Quotation|While crossing the sea from Sagami to Kazusa, Yamato Takeru boasted, “This small sea, I could leap over it.” However, a violent storm arose, and the ship began to drift. Ototachibana-hime, his consort, said, “This storm must be the work of the sea god. Allow me to enter the sea as your substitute.” With these words, she entered the waves, and the storm ceased. The ship safely reached the shore, and the sea was named Tachibana Sea in her honor.宇治谷孟 『日本書紀（上）全現代語訳』 168ページ}} In {{ill|Mobara, Chiba|ja|茂原市}}, {{ill|Tachibana Shrine (Mobara)|ja|橘樹神社}} honors Ototachibana-hime. The shrine is an {{ill|Engishiki|ja|延喜式}}-listed shrine, one of Kazusa's five major shrines, and the only shrine recorded in historical texts to enshrine her. According to legend, Yamato Takeru planted a tachibana tree as her grave marker here.菊池利夫 『東京湾史』 80ページ In the {{ill|Kojiki|ja|古事記}}, Ototachibana-hime offered herself to calm the sea gods’ wrath as Yamato Takeru encountered a storm at Hashirimizu. She placed eight layers of sedge, hide, and silk mats on the waves, sat on them, and entered the sea, calming the waters.次田真幸 『古事記（中）全訳注』 150ページ {{Quotation|She said, “I shall enter the sea as your substitute. Please proceed safely with your campaign.” She then spread eight layers of sedge, hide, and silk mats over the waves, seated herself on them, and entered the sea. The storm subsided, and the ship advanced. As she descended, she sang:
* “In Aibu’s fields, flames rise; standing amid them, you questioned me, my lord.”
Her comb was found washed ashore seven days later, and Yamato Takeru enshrined it, creating her tomb.次田真幸 『古事記（中）全訳注』 147ページ}} The comb is said to be enshrined at Tachibana Shrine, and her poem expresses her gratitude to Yamato Takeru for his care during a previous ordeal. Yamato Takeru’s lament, “Azuma haya” (“My wife!”), upon reaching Azuma, is said to have named the eastern provinces “Azuma,” a geonymic tale.宇治谷孟 『日本書紀（上）全現代語訳』 169ページ次田真幸 『古事記（中）全訳注』 153ページ == Hitachi no Kuni Fudoki == In the {{ill|Hitachi no Kuni Fudoki|ja|常陸国風土記}}, Ototachibana-hime appears as Ōtachibana-hime or Empress Tachibana, while Yamato Takeru is referred to as Emperor Yamato.植垣節也 『風土記 新編日本古典文学全集5』 388ページ == Sendaikujihongi == In the {{ill|Sendaikujihongi|ja|先代旧事本紀}}, Ototachibana-hime is recorded as the mother of Wakatakehiko-no-Mikoto and eight other sons.植垣節也 『風土記 新編日本古典文学全集5』 417ページ == Shrines and Legends == Ototachibana-hime’s legend is central to shrines across Kazusa (modern-day Chiba) and beyond. Shrines such as {{ill|Tachibana Shrine (Kanagawa)|ja|橘樹神社}}, {{ill|Nomura Shrine|ja|能褒野神社}}, and {{ill|Hashirimizu Shrine|ja|走水神社 (横須賀市)}} enshrine her alongside Yamato Takeru. Places like {{ill|Sodegaura|ja|袖ケ浦}} are named after the legend of her sleeves washing ashore. The sacred tachibana tree associated with her suggests she may have been a shaman.小野一之・鈴木彰・谷口榮・樋口州男 『人物伝小辞典 古代・中世編』 54ページ == References == == Bibliography == * 宇治谷孟 『日本書紀（上）全現代語訳』 講談社、1988年 ISBN 4-06-158833-8 * 次田真幸 『古事記（中）全訳注』 講談社、1980年 ISBN 4-06-158208-9 * 植垣節也 『風土記 新編日本古典文学全集5』 小学館、1997年 ISBN 4-09-658005-8 * 菊池利夫 『東京湾史』 大日本図書印刷 1974年 * 小野一之・鈴木彰・谷口榮・樋口州男 『人物伝小辞典 古代・中世編』 東京堂出版 2004年 == Related Topics == * {{ill|Azuma|ja|東#呼称}} * {{ill|Tamasaki Shrine|ja|玉崎神社}} * {{ill|Ane Saki Shrine|ja|姉埼神社}} * {{ill|Soga-hime Shrine|ja|蘇我比咩神社}} * {{ill|Kimisara Tower|ja|きみさらずタワー}} {{Japan mythology}} [[Category:Ototachibana-hime]] [[Category:Women in ancient Japan]] [[Category:Consorts of Yamato Takeru]] [[Category:Legendary Japanese women]] [[Category:Japanese deities (human-related)]] [[Category:Birth and death dates unknown]] [[Category:ZZZZZZZZZ Unlinked]] [[Category:pages needing attribution]][[Category:Ototachibana-hime]][[Category:pages needing attribution]] [[category:unsorted completion categories]] [[Category:attempting to force efresh]] [[Category:bump-01-18]] [[ja:弟橘媛]]

[[File:Ototachibanahime.png|thumb|Ototachibanahime (from ''{{ill|Zenkengojitsu|ja|前賢故実}}'')]] '''Ototachibanahime''' (弟橘媛, birth unknown – 40th year of Emperor Keikō) was, according to the ''{{ill|Nihon Shoki|ja|日本書紀}}'', the consort of {{ill|Yamato Takeru|ja|ヤマトタケル}}. One of the consorts accompanying him, named Ototachibanahime, daughter of Takeoshi Yama Tarine, addressed him, saying, “This storm must be the work of the sea deity. I will take your place and appease the gods.” With those words, she leapt into the sea. The storm subsided immediately, and the ship safely reached shore. The place was later called ''Tachimizu'' in her honor...''Nihon Shoki'' (translated by Mitsuo Ueda), p. 168}} The {{ill|Tachibana Shrine (Mobara)|ja|橘樹神社 (茂原市)}} in {{ill|Mobara|ja|茂原市}}, Chiba Prefecture, is said to be where Yamato Takeru commemorated Ototachibanahime by planting a tachibana (citrus) tree as her grave marker. When a storm arose in the [[Uraga Channel]], she offered herself to calm the sea: {{Quotation|...Then, as they attempted to cross the sea at Hashirimizu, a storm arose. To appease the wrath of the sea deity, Ototachibanahime declared, “I will take my lord’s place to calm the sea and ensure the success of the mission.” She laid eight layers of sedge mats, skin mats, and silk mats on the waves, seated herself on them, and entered the sea. The waves subsided, allowing the ship to proceed.''Kojiki'' (translated by {{ill|Makoto Tsuda|ja|次田真幸}}), p. 150}} Her parting song, only found in the ''Kojiki,'' reflects her gratitude towards Yamato Takeru for his concern during their peril: * ''As the fire blazes on Saho's field, you asked of me amidst the flames. My lord, how kind you are!''''Kojiki'' (translated by Makoto Tsuda), p. 147 == ''Hitachi Fudoki'' Account == In the ''{{ill|Hitachi Fudoki|ja|常陸国風土記}}'', Ototachibanahime appears as ''Otachibanahime no Mikoto'' or ''Tachibana Kōgō'' (Empress Tachibana). Yamato Takeru, referred to as Emperor Yamato Takeru, is depicted reuniting with her in the area now known as Itako and Namegata in Ibaraki Prefecture.''Fudoki,'' {{ill|New Japanese Classic Literature Complete Works|ja|新編日本古典文学全集}}, p. 388 == Shrines and Legends == Shrines dedicated to Ototachibanahime, such as {{ill|Tachibana Shrine (Mobara)|ja|橘樹神社 (茂原市)}} and {{ill|Hashirimizu Shrine|ja|走水神社}}, are located in Chiba Prefecture and other regions. Legends surrounding her, including the origin of place names like ''Sodegaura'' in Chiba and Narashino, testify to her lasting cultural significance. == References == {{Reflist}} == Bibliography == * Mitsuo Ueda, ''Nihon Shoki (Volume I): Full Modern Translation,'' Kodansha, 1988. ISBN 4-06-158833-8 * Makoto Tsuda, ''Kojiki (Volume II): Full Annotated Translation,'' Kodansha, 1980. ISBN 4-06-158208-9 * Toshiwo Kikuchi, ''History of Tokyo Bay,'' Dai-Nihon Printing, 1974 * Ichiyuki Ono et al., ''Biographical Dictionary of Ancient and Medieval Japan,'' Tokyo-do Publishing, 2004. == '''Ototachibana-hime''' (弟橘媛) was the wife of {{ill|Yamato Takeru|ja|ヤマトタケル}}, according to the {{ill|Nihon Shoki|ja|日本書紀}}. In the {{ill|Kojiki|ja|古事記}}, she is referred to as Otachibana-hime no Mikoto (弟橘比売命), the consort of Yamato Takeru. == Ototachibana-hime in the Kojiki and Nihon Shoki == In the {{ill|Nihon Shoki|ja|日本書紀}}, she is described as the daughter of {{ill|Oshiyama-no-Sukune|ja|忍山宿禰}}, an ancestor of the {{ill|Hozumi clan|ja|穂積氏}}. She bore Yamato Takeru a son, {{ill|Wakatakehiko-no-Mikoto|ja|稚武彦王}}.宇治谷孟 『日本書紀（上）全現代語訳』 174ページ {{Quotation|While crossing the sea from Sagami to Kazusa, Yamato Takeru boasted, “This small sea, I could leap over it.” However, a violent storm arose, and the ship began to drift. Ototachibana-hime, his consort, said, “This storm must be the work of the sea god. Allow me to enter the sea as your substitute.” With these words, she entered the waves, and the storm ceased. The ship safely reached the shore, and the sea was named Tachibana Sea in her honor.宇治谷孟 『日本書紀（上）全現代語訳』 168ページ}} In {{ill|Mobara, Chiba|ja|茂原市}}, {{ill|Tachibana Shrine (Mobara)|ja|橘樹神社}} honors Ototachibana-hime. The shrine is an {{ill|Engishiki|ja|延喜式}}-listed shrine, one of Kazusa's five major shrines, and the only shrine recorded in historical texts to enshrine her. According to legend, Yamato Takeru planted a tachibana tree as her grave marker here.菊池利夫 『東京湾史』 80ページ In the {{ill|Kojiki|ja|古事記}}, Ototachibana-hime offered herself to calm the sea gods’ wrath as Yamato Takeru encountered a storm at Hashirimizu. She placed eight layers of sedge, hide, and silk mats on the waves, sat on them, and entered the sea, calming the waters.次田真幸 『古事記（中）全訳注』 150ページ {{Quotation|She said, “I shall enter the sea as your substitute. Please proceed safely with your campaign.” She then spread eight layers of sedge, hide, and silk mats over the waves, seated herself on them, and entered the sea. The storm subsided, and the ship advanced. As she descended, she sang:
* “In Aibu’s fields, flames rise; standing amid them, you questioned me, my lord.”
Her comb was found washed ashore seven days later, and Yamato Takeru enshrined it, creating her tomb.次田真幸 『古事記（中）全訳注』 147ページ}} The comb is said to be enshrined at Tachibana Shrine, and her poem expresses her gratitude to Yamato Takeru for his care during a previous ordeal. Yamato Takeru’s lament, “Azuma haya” (“My wife!”), upon reaching Azuma, is said to have named the eastern provinces “Azuma,” a geonymic tale.宇治谷孟 『日本書紀（上）全現代語訳』 169ページ次田真幸 『古事記（中）全訳注』 153ページ == Hitachi no Kuni Fudoki == In the {{ill|Hitachi no Kuni Fudoki|ja|常陸国風土記}}, Ototachibana-hime appears as Ōtachibana-hime or Empress Tachibana, while Yamato Takeru is referred to as Emperor Yamato.植垣節也 『風土記 新編日本古典文学全集5』 388ページ == Sendaikujihongi == In the {{ill|Sendaikujihongi|ja|先代旧事本紀}}, Ototachibana-hime is recorded as the mother of Wakatakehiko-no-Mikoto and eight other sons.植垣節也 『風土記 新編日本古典文学全集5』 417ページ == Shrines and Legends == Ototachibana-hime’s legend is central to shrines across Kazusa (modern-day Chiba) and beyond. Shrines such as {{ill|Tachibana Shrine (Kanagawa)|ja|橘樹神社}}, {{ill|Nomura Shrine|ja|能褒野神社}}, and {{ill|Hashirimizu Shrine|ja|走水神社 (横須賀市)}} enshrine her alongside Yamato Takeru. Places like {{ill|Sodegaura|ja|袖ケ浦}} are named after the legend of her sleeves washing ashore. The sacred tachibana tree associated with her suggests she may have been a shaman.小野一之・鈴木彰・谷口榮・樋口州男 『人物伝小辞典 古代・中世編』 54ページ == References == == Bibliography == * 宇治谷孟 『日本書紀（上）全現代語訳』 講談社、1988年 ISBN 4-06-158833-8 * 次田真幸 『古事記（中）全訳注』 講談社、1980年 ISBN 4-06-158208-9 * 植垣節也 『風土記 新編日本古典文学全集5』 小学館、1997年 ISBN 4-09-658005-8 * 菊池利夫 『東京湾史』 大日本図書印刷 1974年 * 小野一之・鈴木彰・谷口榮・樋口州男 『人物伝小辞典 古代・中世編』 東京堂出版 2004年 == Related Topics == * {{ill|Azuma|ja|東#呼称}} * {{ill|Tamasaki Shrine|ja|玉崎神社}} * {{ill|Ane Saki Shrine|ja|姉埼神社}} * {{ill|Soga-hime Shrine|ja|蘇我比咩神社}} * {{ill|Kimisara Tower|ja|きみさらずタワー}} {{Japan mythology}} [[Category:Ototachibana-hime]] [[Category:Women in ancient Japan]] [[Category:Consorts of Yamato Takeru]] [[Category:Legendary Japanese women]] [[Category:Japanese deities (human-related)]] [[Category:Birth and death dates unknown]] [[Category:ZZZZZZZZZ Unlinked]] [[Category:pages needing attribution]][[Category:Ototachibana-hime]][[Category:pages needing attribution]] [[category:unsorted completion categories]] [[Category:attempting to force efresh]] [[Category:bump-01-18]] [[ja:弟橘媛]]

[[File:Ototachibanahime.png|thumb|Ototachibanahime (from ''{{ill|Zenkengojitsu|ja|前賢故実}}'')]] '''Ototachibanahime''' (弟橘媛, birth unknown – 40th year of Emperor Keikō) was, according to the ''{{ill|Nihon Shoki|ja|日本書紀}}'', the consort of {{ill|Yamato Takeru|ja|ヤマトタケル}}. In the ''{{ill|Kojiki|ja|古事記}}'', she is referred to as Otachibanahime no Mikoto, the consort of Yamato Takeru. == Ototachibanahime in the Chronicles == According to the ''Nihon Shoki'', she was the daughter of {{ill|Takeoshi Yama Tarine|ja|建忍山垂根}} of the {{ill|Hozumi clan|ja|穂積氏}} and bore a son, {{ill|Prince Wakatarashihiko|ja|稚武彦王}}, with Yamato Takeru.''Nihon Shoki'' (translated by {{ill|Mitsuo Ueda|ja|宇治谷孟}}), p. 174 {{Quotation|...When they reached Sagami and intended to cross to Kazusa, Yamato Takeru declared, “This small sea can be easily leapt over.” However, halfway through the journey, a violent storm arose, and the ship began to drift. One of the consorts accompanying him, named Ototachibanahime, daughter of Takeoshi Yama Tarine, addressed him, saying, “This storm must be the work of the sea deity. I will take your place and appease the gods.” With those words, she leapt into the sea. The storm subsided immediately, and the ship safely reached shore. The place was later called ''Tachimizu'' in her honor...''Nihon Shoki'' (translated by Mitsuo Ueda), p. 168}} The {{ill|Tachibana Shrine (Mobara)|ja|橘樹神社 (茂原市)}} in {{ill|Mobara|ja|茂原市}}, Chiba Prefecture, is said to be where Yamato Takeru commemorated Ototachibanahime by planting a tachibana (citrus) tree as her grave marker. This shrine is a significant site, being listed in the ''Engishiki'' as one of the principal shrines of Kazusa Province and the only shrine officially dedicated to Ototachibanahime. == ''Kojiki'' Narrative == The ''Kojiki'' recounts a similar story of Ototachibanahime's sacrifice during Yamato Takeru's journey. When a storm arose in the [[Uraga Channel]], she offered herself to calm the sea: {{Quotation|...Then, as they attempted to cross the sea at Hashirimizu, a storm arose. To appease the wrath of the sea deity, Ototachibanahime declared, “I will take my lord’s place to calm the sea and ensure the success of the mission.” She laid eight layers of sedge mats, skin mats, and silk mats on the waves, seated herself on them, and entered the sea. The waves subsided, allowing the ship to proceed.''Kojiki'' (translated by {{ill|Makoto Tsuda|ja|次田真幸}}), p. 150}} Her parting song, only found in the ''Kojiki,'' reflects her gratitude towards Yamato Takeru for his concern during their peril: * ''As the fire blazes on Saho's field, you asked of me amidst the flames. My lord, how kind you are!''''Kojiki'' (translated by Makoto Tsuda), p. 147 == ''Hitachi Fudoki'' Account == In the ''{{ill|Hitachi Fudoki|ja|常陸国風土記}}'', Ototachibanahime appears as ''Otachibanahime no Mikoto'' or ''Tachibana Kōgō'' (Empress Tachibana). Yamato Takeru, referred to as Emperor Yamato Takeru, is depicted reuniting with her in the area now known as Itako and Namegata in Ibaraki Prefecture.''Fudoki,'' {{ill|New Japanese Classic Literature Complete Works|ja|新編日本古典文学全集}}, p. 388 == Shrines and Legends == Shrines dedicated to Ototachibanahime, such as {{ill|Tachibana Shrine (Mobara)|ja|橘樹神社 (茂原市)}} and {{ill|Hashirimizu Shrine|ja|走水神社}}, are located in Chiba Prefecture and other regions. Legends surrounding her, including the origin of place names like ''Sodegaura'' in Chiba and Narashino, testify to her lasting cultural significance. == References == {{Reflist}} == Bibliography == * Mitsuo Ueda, ''Nihon Shoki (Volume I): Full Modern Translation,'' Kodansha, 1988. ISBN 4-06-158833-8 * Makoto Tsuda, ''Kojiki (Volume II): Full Annotated Translation,'' Kodansha, 1980. ISBN 4-06-158208-9 * Toshiwo Kikuchi, ''History of Tokyo Bay,'' Dai-Nihon Printing, 1974 * Ichiyuki Ono et al., ''Biographical Dictionary of Ancient and Medieval Japan,'' Tokyo-do Publishing, 2004. == attribution]][[Category:Ototachibana-hime]][[Category:pages needing attribution]] [[category:unsorted completion categories]] [[Category:Unknown birth and death years]] [[ja:弟橘媛]] '''Ototachibana-hime''' (弟橘媛) was the wife of {{ill|Yamato Takeru|ja|ヤマトタケル}}, according to the {{ill|Nihon Shoki|ja|日本書紀}}. In the {{ill|Kojiki|ja|古事記}}, she is referred to as Otachibana-hime no Mikoto (弟橘比売命), the consort of Yamato Takeru. == Ototachibana-hime in the Kojiki and Nihon Shoki == In the {{ill|Nihon Shoki|ja|日本書紀}}, she is described as the daughter of {{ill|Oshiyama-no-Sukune|ja|忍山宿禰}}, an ancestor of the {{ill|Hozumi clan|ja|穂積氏}}. She bore Yamato Takeru a son, {{ill|Wakatakehiko-no-Mikoto|ja|稚武彦王}}.宇治谷孟 『日本書紀（上）全現代語訳』 174ページ {{Quotation|While crossing the sea from Sagami to Kazusa, Yamato Takeru boasted, “This small sea, I could leap over it.” However, a violent storm arose, and the ship began to drift. Ototachibana-hime, his consort, said, “This storm must be the work of the sea god. Allow me to enter the sea as your substitute.” With these words, she entered the waves, and the storm ceased. The ship safely reached the shore, and the sea was named Tachibana Sea in her honor.宇治谷孟 『日本書紀（上）全現代語訳』 168ページ}} In {{ill|Mobara, Chiba|ja|茂原市}}, {{ill|Tachibana Shrine (Mobara)|ja|橘樹神社}} honors Ototachibana-hime. The shrine is an {{ill|Engishiki|ja|延喜式}}-listed shrine, one of Kazusa's five major shrines, and the only shrine recorded in historical texts to enshrine her. According to legend, Yamato Takeru planted a tachibana tree as her grave marker here.菊池利夫 『東京湾史』 80ページ In the {{ill|Kojiki|ja|古事記}}, Ototachibana-hime offered herself to calm the sea gods’ wrath as Yamato Takeru encountered a storm at Hashirimizu. She placed eight layers of sedge, hide, and silk mats on the waves, sat on them, and entered the sea, calming the waters.次田真幸 『古事記（中）全訳注』 150ページ {{Quotation|She said, “I shall enter the sea as your substitute. Please proceed safely with your campaign.” She then spread eight layers of sedge, hide, and silk mats over the waves, seated herself on them, and entered the sea. The storm subsided, and the ship advanced. As she descended, she sang:
* “In Aibu’s fields, flames rise; standing amid them, you questioned me, my lord.”
Her comb was found washed ashore seven days later, and Yamato Takeru enshrined it, creating her tomb.次田真幸 『古事記（中）全訳注』 147ページ}} The comb is said to be enshrined at Tachibana Shrine, and her poem expresses her gratitude to Yamato Takeru for his care during a previous ordeal. Yamato Takeru’s lament, “Azuma haya” (“My wife!”), upon reaching Azuma, is said to have named the eastern provinces “Azuma,” a geonymic tale.宇治谷孟 『日本書紀（上）全現代語訳』 169ページ次田真幸 『古事記（中）全訳注』 153ページ == Hitachi no Kuni Fudoki == In the {{ill|Hitachi no Kuni Fudoki|ja|常陸国風土記}}, Ototachibana-hime appears as Ōtachibana-hime or Empress Tachibana, while Yamato Takeru is referred to as Emperor Yamato.植垣節也 『風土記 新編日本古典文学全集5』 388ページ == Sendaikujihongi == In the {{ill|Sendaikujihongi|ja|先代旧事本紀}}, Ototachibana-hime is recorded as the mother of Wakatakehiko-no-Mikoto and eight other sons.植垣節也 『風土記 新編日本古典文学全集5』 417ページ == Shrines and Legends == Ototachibana-hime’s legend is central to shrines across Kazusa (modern-day Chiba) and beyond. Shrines such as {{ill|Tachibana Shrine (Kanagawa)|ja|橘樹神社}}, {{ill|Nomura Shrine|ja|能褒野神社}}, and {{ill|Hashirimizu Shrine|ja|走水神社 (横須賀市)}} enshrine her alongside Yamato Takeru. Places like {{ill|Sodegaura|ja|袖ケ浦}} are named after the legend of her sleeves washing ashore. The sacred tachibana tree associated with her suggests she may have been a shaman.小野一之・鈴木彰・谷口榮・樋口州男 『人物伝小辞典 古代・中世編』 54ページ == References == == Bibliography == * 宇治谷孟 『日本書紀（上）全現代語訳』 講談社、1988年 ISBN 4-06-158833-8 * 次田真幸 『古事記（中）全訳注』 講談社、1980年 ISBN 4-06-158208-9 * 植垣節也 『風土記 新編日本古典文学全集5』 小学館、1997年 ISBN 4-09-658005-8 * 菊池利夫 『東京湾史』 大日本図書印刷 1974年 * 小野一之・鈴木彰・谷口榮・樋口州男 『人物伝小辞典 古代・中世編』 東京堂出版 2004年 == Related Topics == * {{ill|Azuma|ja|東#呼称}} * {{ill|Tamasaki Shrine|ja|玉崎神社}} * {{ill|Ane Saki Shrine|ja|姉埼神社}} * {{ill|Soga-hime Shrine|ja|蘇我比咩神社}} * {{ill|Kimisara Tower|ja|きみさらずタワー}} {{Japan mythology}} [[Category:Ototachibana-hime]] [[Category:Women in ancient Japan]] [[Category:Consorts of Yamato Takeru]] [[Category:Legendary Japanese women]] [[Category:Japanese deities (human-related)]] [[Category:Birth and death dates unknown]] [[Category:ZZZZZZZZZ Unlinked]] [[Category:pages needing attribution]][[Category:Ototachibana-hime]][[Category:pages needing attribution]] [[category:unsorted completion categories]] [[Category:attempting to force efresh]] [[Category:bump-01-18]] [[Category:前賢故実]] [[ja:弟橘媛]]

[[File:Ototachibanahime.png|thumb|Ototachibanahime (from ''{{ill|Zenkengojitsu|ja|前賢故実}}'')]] '''Ototachibanahime''' (弟橘媛, birth unknown – 40th year of Emperor Keikō) was, according to the ''{{ill|Nihon Shoki|ja|日本書紀}}'', the consort of {{ill|Yamato Takeru|ja|ヤマトタケル}}. In the ''{{ill|Kojiki|ja|古事記}}'', she is referred to as Otachibanahime no Mikoto, the consort of Yamato Takeru. == Ototachibanahime in the Chronicles == According to the ''Nihon Shoki'', she was the daughter of {{ill|Takeoshi Yama Tarine|ja|建忍山垂根}} of the {{ill|Hozumi clan|ja|穂積氏}} and bore a son, {{ill|Prince Wakatarashihiko|ja|稚武彦王}}, with Yamato Takeru.''Nihon Shoki'' (translated by {{ill|Mitsuo Ueda|ja|宇治谷孟}}), p. 174 {{Quotation|...When they reached Sagami and intended to cross to Kazusa, Yamato Takeru declared, “This small sea can be easily leapt over.” However, halfway through the journey, a violent storm arose, and the ship began to drift. One of the consorts accompanying him, named Ototachibanahime, daughter of Takeoshi Yama Tarine, addressed him, saying, “This storm must be the work of the sea deity. I will take your place and appease the gods.” With those words, she leapt into the sea. The storm subsided immediately, and the ship safely reached shore. The place was later called ''Tachimizu'' in her honor...''Nihon Shoki'' (translated by Mitsuo Ueda), p. 168}} The {{ill|Tachibana Shrine (Mobara)|ja|橘樹神社 (茂原市)}} in {{ill|Mobara|ja|茂原市}}, Chiba Prefecture, is said to be where Yamato Takeru commemorated Ototachibanahime by planting a tachibana (citrus) tree as her grave marker. This shrine is a significant site, being listed in the ''Engishiki'' as one of the principal shrines of Kazusa Province and the only shrine officially dedicated to Ototachibanahime. == ''Kojiki'' Narrative == The ''Kojiki'' recounts a similar story of Ototachibanahime's sacrifice during Yamato Takeru's journey. When a storm arose in the [[Uraga Channel]], she offered herself to calm the sea: {{Quotation|...Then, as they attempted to cross the sea at Hashirimizu, a storm arose. To appease the wrath of the sea deity, Ototachibanahime declared, “I will take my lord’s place to calm the sea and ensure the success of the mission.” She laid eight layers of sedge mats, skin mats, and silk mats on the waves, seated herself on them, and entered the sea. The waves subsided, allowing the ship to proceed.''Kojiki'' (translated by {{ill|Makoto Tsuda|ja|次田真幸}}), p. 150}} Her parting song, only found in the ''Kojiki,'' reflects her gratitude towards Yamato Takeru for his concern during their peril: * ''As the fire blazes on Saho's field, you asked of me amidst the flames. My lord, how kind you are!''''Kojiki'' (translated by Makoto Tsuda), p. 147 == ''Hitachi Fudoki'' Account == In the ''{{ill|Hitachi Fudoki|ja|常陸国風土記}}'', Ototachibanahime appears as ''Otachibanahime no Mikoto'' or ''Tachibana Kōgō'' (Empress Tachibana). Yamato Takeru, referred to as Emperor Yamato Takeru, is depicted reuniting with her in the area now known as Itako and Namegata in Ibaraki Prefecture.''Fudoki,'' {{ill|New Japanese Classic Literature Complete Works|ja|新編日本古典文学全集}}, p. 388 == Shrines and Legends == Shrines dedicated to Ototachibanahime, such as {{ill|Tachibana Shrine (Mobara)|ja|橘樹神社 (茂原市)}} and {{ill|Hashirimizu Shrine|ja|走水神社}}, are located in Chiba Prefecture and other regions. Legends surrounding her, including the origin of place names like ''Sodegaura'' in Chiba and Narashino, testify to her lasting cultural significance. == References == {{Reflist}} == Bibliography == * Mitsuo Ueda, ''Nihon Shoki (Volume I): Full Modern Translation,'' Kodansha, 1988. ISBN 4-06-158833-8 * Makoto Tsuda, ''Kojiki (Volume II): Full Annotated Translation,'' Kodansha, 1980. ISBN 4-06-158208-9 * Toshiwo Kikuchi, ''History of Tokyo Bay,'' Dai-Nihon Printing, 1974 * Ichiyuki Ono et al., ''Biographical Dictionary of Ancient and Medieval Japan,'' Tokyo-do Publishing, 2004. == See Also == * {{ill|Azuma|ja|東#呼称}} * {{ill|Tamasaki Shrine|ja|玉崎神社}} * {{ill|Ane Saki Shrine|ja|姉埼神社}} * {{ill|Soga Hime Shrine|ja|蘇我比咩神社}} * {{ill|Kisarazu Tower|ja|きみさらずタワー}} [[Category:ZZZZZZZZZ Unlinked]] [[Category:Ototachibana-hime]][[category:unsorted completion categories]] [[Category:ZZZZZZZZZ Unlinked]] [[Category:Ototachibana-hime]][[category:unsorted completion categories]] The storm subsided, and the ship advanced. As she descended, she sang:

[[File:Ototachibanahime.png|thumb|Ototachibanahime (from ''{{ill|Zenkengojitsu|ja|前賢故実}}'')]] '''Ototachibanahime''' (弟橘媛, birth unknown – 40th year of Emperor Keikō) was, according to the ''{{ill|Nihon Shoki|ja|日本書紀}}'', the consort of {{ill|Yamato Takeru|ja|ヤマトタケル}}. I will take your place and appease the gods.” With those words, she leapt into the sea. The storm subsided immediately, and the ship safely reached shore. When a storm arose in the [[Uraga Channel]], she offered herself to calm the sea: {{Quotation|...Then, as they attempted to cross the sea at Hashirimizu, a storm arose. To appease the wrath of the sea deity, Ototachibanahime declared, “I will take my lord’s place to calm the sea and ensure the success of the mission.” She laid eight layers of sedge mats, skin mats, and silk mats on the waves, seated herself on them, and entered the sea. The waves subsided, allowing the ship to proceed.''Kojiki'' (translated by {{ill|Makoto Tsuda|ja|次田真幸}}), p. 150}} Her parting song, only found in the ''Kojiki,'' reflects her gratitude towards Yamato Takeru for his concern during their peril: * ''As the fire blazes on Saho's field, you asked of me amidst the flames. My lord, how kind you are!''''Kojiki'' (translated by Makoto Tsuda), p. 147 == ''Hitachi Fudoki'' Account == In the ''{{ill|Hitachi Fudoki|ja|常陸国風土記}}'', Ototachibanahime appears as ''Otachibanahime no Mikoto'' or ''Tachibana Kōgō'' (Empress Tachibana). Yamato Takeru, referred to as Emperor Yamato Takeru, is depicted reuniting with her in the area now known as Itako and Namegata in Ibaraki Prefecture.''Fudoki,'' {{ill|New Japanese Classic Literature Complete Works|ja|新編日本古典文学全集}}, p. 388 == Shrines and Legends == Shrines dedicated to Ototachibanahime, such as {{ill|Tachibana Shrine (Mobara)|ja|橘樹神社 (茂原市)}} and {{ill|Hashirimizu Shrine|ja|走水神社}}, are located in Chiba Prefecture and other regions. Legends surrounding her, including the origin of place names like ''Sodegaura'' in Chiba and Narashino, testify to her lasting cultural significance. == References == {{Reflist}} == Bibliography == * Mitsuo Ueda, ''Nihon Shoki (Volume I): Full Modern Translation,'' Kodansha, 1988. ISBN 4-06-158833-8 * Makoto Tsuda, ''Kojiki (Volume II): Full Annotated Translation,'' Kodansha, 1980. ISBN 4-06-158208-9 * Toshiwo Kikuchi, ''History of Tokyo Bay,'' Dai-Nihon Printing, 1974 * Ichiyuki Ono et al., ''Biographical Dictionary of Ancient and Medieval Japan,'' Tokyo-do Publishing, 2004. == See Also == * {{ill|Azuma|ja|東#呼称}} * {{ill|Tamasaki Shrine|ja|玉崎神社}} * {{ill|Ane Saki Shrine|ja|姉埼神社}} * {{ill|Soga Hime Shrine|ja|蘇我比咩神社}} * {{ill|Kisarazu Tower|ja|きみさらずタワー}}
